section.title: The Oracle against Moab
section.sectionRationale: This movement completes the arc begun with Babylon and Assyria by turning to Moab, Israel's kindred but often hostile neighbor east of the Dead Sea. Unusually, the oracle is soaked in the prophet's own tears rather than mere denunciation, pictures Moab's refugees begging Judah for shelter, and briefly holds out David's throne established in mercy, before Moab's proud boasting is judged as empty and its glory reduced within a set three years.
section.thesis: Even in pronouncing real judgment on Moab, the LORD's prophet weeps for its devastation, and Moab's only true hope, refuge under the house of David established in steadfast love, is precisely what its own pride refuses to seek, leaving its empty boasting to end in the swift, dated collapse the LORD has spoken.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the sudden devastation of Moab's cities and the prophet's own weeping; the plea for refuge and the throne established in mercy under David's line; and the judgment on Moab's pride, dated precisely to three years.

units.label: Moab devastated, and Isaiah weeps
units.note: my heart cries out over Moab

verses.text:
This is the burden against Moab: 
Ar in Moab is ruined, destroyed in a night! Kir in Moab is devastated, destroyed in a night!

Dibon goes up to its temple to weep at its high places. Moab wails over Nebo, as well as over Medeba. 
Every head is shaved, every beard is cut off.

In its streets they wear sackcloth; on the rooftops and in the public squares 
they all wail, falling down weeping.
Heshbon and Elealeh cry out; their voices are heard as far as Jahaz. Therefore the soldiers of Moab cry out; their souls tremble within.

My heart cries out over Moab; her fugitives flee as far as Zoar, 
as far as Eglath-shelishiyah. With weeping they ascend the slope of Luhith; they lament their destruction on the road to Horonaim.
The waters of Nimrim are dried up, and the grass is withered; the vegetation is gone, and the greenery is no more.
So they carry their wealth and belongings over the Brook of the Willows.

For their outcry echoes to the border of Moab. Their wailing reaches Eglaim; 
it is heard in Beer-elim.
The waters of Dimon are full of blood, but I will bring more upon Dimon, a lion upon the fugitives of Moab and upon the remnant of the land.
Send the tribute lambs to the ruler of the land, from Sela in the desert to the mount of Daughter Zion.
Like fluttering birds pushed out of the nest, so are the daughters of Moab at the fords of the Arnon:
“Give us counsel; render a decision. Shelter us at noonday with shade as dark as night. Hide the refugees; do not betray the one who flees.

Let my fugitives stay with you; be a refuge for Moab from the destroyer.” 
When the oppressor has gone, destruction has ceased, and the oppressors have vanished from the land,
in loving devotion a throne will be established in the tent of David. A judge seeking {{mishpat5|justice}} and hastening righteousness will sit on it in faithfulness.
We have heard of Moab's pomposity, his exceeding pride and conceit, his overflowing arrogance. But his boasting is empty.
Therefore let Moab wail; let them wail together for Moab. Moan for the raisin cakes of Kir-hareseth, you who are utterly stricken.

For the fields of Heshbon have withered, along with the grapevines of Sibmah. The rulers of the nations have trampled its choicest vines, which had reached as far as Jazer and spread toward the desert. Their shoots had spread out and passed over the sea.
So I weep with Jazer for the vines of Sibmah; I drench Heshbon and Elealeh with my tears. Triumphant shouts have fallen silent over your summer fruit and your harvest.
Joy and gladness are removed from the orchard; no one sings or shouts in the vineyards. No one tramples the grapes in the winepresses; I have put an end to the cheering.
Therefore my heart laments for Moab like a harp, my inmost being for Kir-heres.
When Moab appears on the high place, when he wearies himself and enters his sanctuary to pray, it will do him no good.
This is the message that the LORD spoke earlier concerning Moab.

And now the LORD says, “In three years, as a hired worker counts the years, Moab's splendor will become an object of contempt, with all her many people. And those who are left will be few and feeble.”

mishpat5.gloss: Justice, right judgment. Even within an oracle of judgment against a foreign nation, this word surfaces as the description of the ideal Davidic ruler, a judge who seeks justice, tying Moab's only possible hope of refuge directly to the same righteous kingship promised throughout Isaiah for Israel and, ultimately, all nations.

basic.context: This oracle pronounces devastating judgment on Moab, Israel's neighbor and distant relative east of the Dead Sea, cataloging city after city falling into mourning, wailing, and flight. Remarkably, the prophet himself is moved to tears over this judgment, not merely announcing it with detachment, and the oracle briefly holds out an actual path to safety, refuge under a coming Davidic ruler who governs in justice and mercy, before returning to describe Moab's proud refusal of that path and its consequent, precisely dated collapse within three years.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real, historically anchored judgment oracle against an actual neighboring nation, yet it is unusual among the oracles against the nations for its genuine, repeated expressions of prophetic grief, showing that pronouncing deserved judgment and feeling real compassion for those judged are not contradictory. The honest question this passage raises is why Moab, offered actual refuge under David's throne, ends up judged anyway; the answer given directly in 16:6 is that Moab's own pride and empty boasting kept it from actually seeking the shelter that was genuinely available.
basic.originalLanguages: Even in the middle of describing devastation, the oracle names mishpat, justice, as the defining mark of the coming Davidic ruler who could have offered Moab genuine refuge, tying this foreign nation's hope directly to the same righteous kingship theme running throughout Isaiah. The repeated Hebrew verb for weeping and wailing, used of both Moab's own people and of the prophet himself, blurs the line between judge and mourner throughout the passage.
basic.typology: The throne established in mercy under David's line, offering refuge to fleeing foreigners, anticipates the wider biblical pattern of God's kingdom extending shelter to Gentiles who come in humility, fulfilled fully when Christ, David's greater Son, welcomes people of every nation into His kingdom. Moab's proud refusal of available refuge anticipates the New Testament's warnings that pride, not lack of opportunity, is what ultimately keeps people from the salvation genuinely offered them.
basic.christ: The Davidic throne offered as refuge here, established in mercy with a judge who seeks justice and hastens righteousness, finds its full and final fulfillment in Christ, the true Son of David whose kingdom offers actual shelter to all who come to Him in humility, regardless of national origin, exactly as Ruth the Moabitess herself was grafted into David's own line, Ruth 4:13-17, Matthew 1:5. Isaiah's own tears over judged Moab foreshadow Christ's own weeping over Jerusalem, Luke 19:41, showing that God's justice and God's compassion are never at odds, even when judgment must fall.
basic.god: God's judgment on nations is real and serious, yet it is never detached from compassion; the prophet's own tears model a heart that can pronounce deserved judgment while genuinely grieving over its human cost. God also genuinely offers refuge even to a hostile, judged nation, showing His justice always includes a real, if often refused, invitation to mercy.
basic.crossReferences: Ruth 4:13-17 shows a Moabite woman actually welcomed into David's own line generations before this oracle, proof that ethnic origin was never the barrier; pride and unbelief were. Luke 19:41 has Jesus weep over Jerusalem's coming judgment much as Isaiah weeps here over Moab's. Zephaniah 2:8-11 pronounces similar judgment on Moab for its pride and mockery of God's people. Matthew 1:5 lists Ruth the Moabitess in Jesus's own genealogy, a living testimony that God's grace reaches even nations under judgment.
basic.oneStory: This oracle shows both the reality of deserved judgment and the depth of God's compassion even toward those He judges, offering genuine refuge that pride alone refuses. That refuge, a throne established in mercy under David's line, is fulfilled in Christ, whose own ancestry includes a Moabite woman, proof that no nation's history of judgment forecloses the invitation to find shelter in Him.

context.literary: The oracle is unusually marked by first-person prophetic grief, 'my heart cries out over Moab,' 15:5, and 'I weep with Jazer... I drench Heshbon and Elealeh with my tears,' 16:9, a striking departure from the more detached tone of surrounding oracles against the nations. The brief throne-of-David oracle in 16:3-5 interrupts the surrounding lament almost like a window of hope before the narrative returns to describe Moab's pride and its consequences.
context.historical: Moab, located east of the Dead Sea, was a kindred nation to Israel through Lot, Genesis 19:37, with a long, often hostile relationship, alternating between vassalage and rebellion against Israel and Judah throughout the biblical period. The specific city-by-city devastation described likely reflects an Assyrian campaign, though the precise historical incident is debated; the dating formula in 16:14, 'in three years,' functions similarly to other precisely timed prophecies in Isaiah.
context.canonical: Moab's story runs throughout Scripture from Lot's daughters in Genesis 19, through Balaam's failed curse in Numbers 22-24, to Ruth the Moabitess joining Israel and David's own line in the book of Ruth, making this oracle of judgment particularly poignant given that God's mercy had already, generations earlier, welcomed a Moabite woman into the very Davidic line offered here as refuge.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To pronounce genuine, deserved judgment on Moab's coming devastation while modeling authentic prophetic grief rather than cold detachment, and to show that real refuge was available to Moab under David's merciful throne, a refuge Moab's own pride kept it from seeking.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes a specific historical judgment on a specific ancient nation; it does not prescribe how believers should regard any particular contemporary nation as under identical divine judgment. The enduring principles are that judgment and compassion can coexist in a godly heart, and that pride, not a lack of available mercy, is what most often keeps people from the refuge God offers.

debates.question: Whose voice is weeping in verses like 15:5 and 16:9, Isaiah's own compassion, or God's own grief expressed through the prophet?

views.label: The prophet's own genuine, personal grief
views.case: Prophets elsewhere express personal emotional responses to the messages they deliver, Jeremiah being called the weeping prophet, so this may reflect Isaiah's own compassionate reaction to pronouncing such devastating judgment on a neighboring people.

views.label: God's own grief expressed through the prophet's voice
views.case: Since the oracle is introduced as the LORD's own burden and message, 15:1, 16:13-14, the first-person weeping may represent God's own compassionate heart being voiced through His prophet, consistent with how God elsewhere expresses grief over judgment He must nonetheless bring, Ezekiel 33:11.

debates.bearing: Either reading affirms the same core truth: genuine judgment and genuine compassion are not contradictory but coexist fully, whether we understand the tears as the prophet's own faithful reflection of God's heart or as God's heart itself voiced directly through him.

study.originalLanguages: In the midst of describing devastation, the oracle names mishpat, justice, as the defining mark of the coming Davidic ruler who could have offered Moab genuine shelter, tying this foreign nation's only real hope directly to the same righteous kingship theme woven throughout Isaiah. The recurring Hebrew language of weeping and wailing applies both to Moab's own devastated people and to the prophet's own voice, blurring any easy line between detached judge and grieving mourner throughout the oracle.

typology.type: The throne established in mercy, offering refuge to fleeing foreigners, Isaiah 16:3-5
typology.fulfillment: Christ's kingdom, welcoming people of every nation who come to Him in humility, just as Ruth the Moabitess was welcomed into David's own line, Ruth 4:13-17.

typology.type: Isaiah's tears over judged Moab, Isaiah 15:5, 16:9
typology.fulfillment: Christ's own weeping over Jerusalem's coming judgment, Luke 19:41, showing that God's justice and compassion are never at odds.

study.christ: The Davidic throne offered here as refuge, established in mercy with a judge who seeks justice and hastens righteousness, finds its full and final fulfillment in Christ, the true Son of David, whose kingdom offers actual shelter to all who come to Him in humility, regardless of national origin, precisely as Ruth the Moabitess herself was welcomed generations earlier into David's own line, Ruth 4:13-17, Matthew 1:5. Isaiah's own tears over judged Moab foreshadow Christ's weeping over Jerusalem's coming judgment, Luke 19:41, proof that God's justice and God's compassion are never opposed, even when real judgment must fall.
study.god: God's judgment on nations is real and serious, never merely symbolic, yet it is never detached from compassion; the prophet's own tears model a heart that can announce deserved judgment while genuinely grieving its human cost. God also genuinely offers refuge even to a hostile, judged people, showing that His justice always carries within it a real, if frequently refused, invitation to mercy.

crossReferences.note: A Moabite woman actually welcomed into David's own royal line generations before this oracle, proof that ethnic origin was never the true barrier to God's mercy.
Jesus weeps over Jerusalem's coming judgment much as Isaiah weeps here over Moab's devastation.
Pronounces a similar later judgment on Moab specifically for its pride and mockery of God's people.
Lists Ruth the Moabitess in Jesus's own genealogy, living testimony that God's grace reaches even nations under judgment.

study.oneStory: This oracle shows both the full reality of deserved judgment and the depth of God's compassion even toward those He judges, offering genuine refuge that pride alone ultimately refuses. That refuge, a throne established in mercy under David's line, is fulfilled in Christ, whose own ancestry includes a Moabite woman, proof that no nation's history under judgment forecloses the invitation to find lasting shelter in Him.

quiz.q: What is unusual about the prophet's own tone in pronouncing this judgment on Moab?

options.text: Isaiah repeatedly expresses his own genuine grief and tears over Moab's devastation, rather than detached denunciation.
options.note: Phrases like 'my heart cries out over Moab' and 'I weep with Jazer' are distinctive within these oracles against the nations.

options.text: Isaiah expresses joy and celebration over Moab's fall.
options.note: The tone is one of genuine sorrow and weeping, not celebration.

options.text: Isaiah refuses to pronounce any judgment on Moab at all.
options.note: The judgment is pronounced clearly and repeatedly; what is unusual is the accompanying grief, not an absence of judgment.

options.text: Isaiah blames Judah, not Moab, for the coming devastation.
options.note: The oracle is explicitly against Moab and its own pride, not primarily an indictment of Judah.

quiz.q: What refuge does 16:3-5 briefly offer to Moab's fleeing refugees?

options.text: Shelter under a throne established in mercy in David's line, governed by a judge who seeks justice.
options.note: This is a real, offered path to safety within the very oracle announcing judgment.

options.text: A permanent military alliance with Assyria.
options.note: The refuge offered is specifically under David's throne, not an Assyrian alliance.

options.text: Full independence and self-rule for Moab.
options.note: The offer is refuge and shelter under Judah's Davidic throne, not Moabite independence.

options.text: Relocation to Egypt.
options.note: The refuge described is specifically 'in the tent of David,' not Egypt.

quiz.q: Why, according to 16:6, does Moab ultimately fail to find lasting safety despite the refuge offered?

options.text: Moab's own pride, conceit, and empty boasting kept it from actually seeking that shelter.
options.note: The text explicitly names pomposity, pride, conceit, and arrogance as the barrier, not any lack of available mercy.

options.text: The offer of refuge was never actually genuine.
options.note: The offer in 16:3-5 reads as a real, if brief, window of genuine hope, not a false promise.

options.text: Moab was geographically too far away to reach Judah.
options.note: The text focuses on pride as the barrier, not geographic distance.

options.text: Judah actively refused to shelter any Moabite refugees.
options.note: The text portrays Judah as being asked for refuge; the failure named is Moab's own pride, not Judah's refusal.

quiz.q: How does Ruth the Moabitess's story relate to this oracle's themes?

options.text: She was actually welcomed into David's own line generations earlier, proving ethnicity was never the true barrier to God's mercy.
options.note: Ruth 4:13-17 and Matthew 1:5 show a Moabite woman fully included in the Messianic line, in striking contrast to Moab's pride here.

options.text: She represents Moab's total and permanent exclusion from God's people.
options.note: Ruth's story shows the opposite: full inclusion and welcome into Israel and David's own family line.

options.text: She has no connection to Isaiah's message about Moab at all.
options.note: Her story provides a striking, directly relevant contrast to Moab's pride-driven refusal of refuge in this oracle.

options.text: She was a queen who ruled over Moab and Judah jointly.
options.note: Ruth was a widow who joined Naomi and later married Boaz, becoming David's great-grandmother, not a ruling queen.

quiz.q: What precise timeframe does 16:14 give for Moab's coming humiliation?

options.text: Three years, counted as precisely as a hired worker counts his years of labor.
options.note: This specific, dated prophecy resembles other precisely timed predictions elsewhere in Isaiah, like the naming of Maher-shalal-hash-baz.

options.text: An immediate, same-day judgment.
options.note: The text specifies a three-year timeframe, not an immediate event.

options.text: Seventy years, echoing the later Babylonian exile timeframe.
options.note: The seventy-year figure belongs to a different, later prophecy regarding Babylon, not this three-year Moab oracle.

options.text: An indefinite, unspecified future period.
options.note: The text is unusually specific, naming three years exactly, not leaving the timing vague.

questions: Isaiah wept even while pronouncing deserved judgment on Moab. Where do you need to hold together honesty about real consequences with genuine compassion for those experiencing them?

Moab was offered real refuge under David's throne but its own pride kept it from seeking that shelter. Where might pride be keeping you from actually receiving help or grace that is genuinely available to you?

Ruth the Moabitess was welcomed fully into David's own family line despite her nation's history under judgment. How does her story encourage you regarding your own past or background that you may feel disqualifies you from God's grace?

The three-year timeframe shows God's judgments are not vague threats but specific and certain. How does that same certainty about God's word encourage you regarding promises He has made that you are still waiting to see fulfilled?

This oracle holds together real judgment and real compassion in a single prophetic voice. Who in your life is under some form of deserved consequence right now, and how can you extend genuine compassion to them without minimizing the reality of what they are facing?

translationNotes.note: 'Destroyed in a night' emphasizes the suddenness of Moab's coming devastation, likely referring to a swift military campaign rather than a single literal overnight event.

'Send the tribute lambs' reflects Moab's historical practice of paying tribute to Judah in livestock, referenced also in 2 Kings 3:4, showing an established, if strained, political relationship between the two nations.

'As a hired worker counts the years' suggests an exact, non-negotiable timeframe, since a hired laborer counts his contracted term precisely rather than loosely, emphasizing the certainty and specificity of this three-year prophecy.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from the Makor scripture toolkit for Isaiah 15:1-16:14.
